verbatim_text: So what if our research agents aren't even very good at doing research within domains that we're familiar with, like machine learning? And if they don't work for machine learning, I would argue that that's kind of a prerequisite for getting these research agents to work in the real world for diverse scientific tasks. If you just take agents that don't work for machine learning and you adjust their data and whatnot and deploy them for different scientific tasks, you're still going to, they're still probably not going to work. And on top of that, you're going to have a lot of other challenges. Like you're going to need to understand the science of this downstream scientific task, like nuclear fusion or something like that, where you otherwise would have that kind of information in machine learning. And so that's why we developed ML Gym. So in ML Gym, we evaluate these machine learning agents on diverse machine learning type problems. So we include things like classical CS problems, things in game theory, computer vision, NLP, and RL. So one of the things that we proposed in this paper is sort of a hierarchy of what the future might look like for research agents. And so at the very bottom of this hierarchy, we might have a research agent that can reproduce a paper that it takes us and put a paper in, it can maybe run some experiments and reproduce the results in the paper. The next level is it takes the baseline from the paper and it can maybe improve a little bit on the baseline. The next level is that it improves upon all baselines in existence. It gets the state of the art result. And then the next and more powerful research agent would be things like producing a novel scientific contribution.
